perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt of a Termination Notice, Recipient shall, within twenty (20) calendar days, either: (i) return the Confidential Materials in its possession to Premisys and instruct its Representatives to do the same; (ii) destroy the Confidential Materials in a secure manner and instruct its Representatives to do the same; or (iii) at Premisys' election, take any combination of steps (i) and (ii).
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of a Termination Notice, Recipient shall, within ninety (90) calendar days, either: (i) return the Confidential Materials in its possession to Premisys and instruct its Representatives to do the same; (ii) destroy the Confidential Materials in a secure manner and instruct its Representatives to do the same; or (iii) at Premisys' election, take any combination of steps (i) and (ii).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return/destruction timeframe to 90 days could conflict with California's reasonable timeframe requirement for returning confidential information in certain circumstances, particularly if the information is highly sensitive or time-dependent.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires responses to requests for document production within 30 days which is shorter than the 90 days stipulated, potentially conflicting with discovery timelines.

CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 2. Response to Inspection Demand [2031.210 - 2031.320] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.280. (a) Any documents or category of documents produced in response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling shall be identified with the specific request number to which the documents respond. (b) The documents shall be produced on the date specified in the demand pursuant to paragraph (2) of subdivision (c) of Section 2031.030, unless an objection has been made to that date. If the date for inspection has been extended pursuant to Section 2031.270, the documents shall be produced on the date agreed to pursuant to that section. (c) If a party responding to a demand for production of electronically stored information objects to a specified form for producing the infor
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the timeframe for returning or destroying confidential materials from 20 to 90 days. The Law Explanation suggests that this conflicts with California law, which requires responses to requests for document production within 30 days. Although the scraped law snippet (CCP 2031.280) pertains to the production of documents in response to a demand for inspection, it establishes a framework for timely document production under California law. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that the 90-day timeframe could conflict with California's reasonable timeframe requirement, especially for sensitive information. Therefore, the extension to 90 days could hinder compliance with legal discovery obligations or other requirements for timely action.

perturbation.original_text: Beginning on Effective Date and continuing for a period of six months thereafter, Recipient will not, either for itself or for any other person or entity, directly or indirectly, solicit, induce or attempt to induce any employee of Premisys to terminate his or her employment with Premisys.
perturbation.changed_text: Beginning on Effective Date and continuing for a period of one month thereafter, Recipient will not, either for itself or for any other person or entity, directly or indirectly, solicit, induce or attempt to induce any employee of Premisys to terminate his or her employment with Premisys.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to one month may be considered unreasonably short and unenforceable under California law, particularly if the company invests heavily in its employees and requires a longer period to protect its interests.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: While California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, the modified one-month restriction on soliciting employees can be seen as an unreasonable restraint on trade if that period is insufficient to protect trade secrets or other legitimate business interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the non-solicitation period from six months to one month, which could be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade under California law, especially if it doesn't adequately protect the company's interests or trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (BPC 16600(a)) states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," unless an exception applies. The Law Explanation argues that a one-month restriction might be insufficient, potentially violating this section. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition specifies that the contradiction exists when 'a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal,' which applies here.

perturbation.original_text: Beginning on the Effective Date and continuing for a period of one (1) year thereafter, neither Recipient nor any of its affiliates will (and neither party nor any of its affiliates will assist or encourage others to), directly or indirectly, unless specifically requested to do so in writing in advance by Premisys' Board of Directors: (a) acquire or agree, offer, seek or propose to acquire, or cause to be acquired, ownership (including, but not limited to, beneficial ownership as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "EXCHANGE ACT")) of any of Premisys' assets
perturbation.changed_text: Beginning on the Effective Date and continuing for a period of ten (10) years thereafter, neither Recipient nor any of its affiliates will (and neither party nor any of its affiliates will assist or encourage others to), directly or indirectly, unless specifically requested to do so in writing in advance by Premisys' Board of Directors: (a) acquire or agree, offer, seek or propose to acquire, or cause to be acquired, ownership (including, but not limited to, beneficial ownership as defined in Rule 13d-3 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "EXCHANGE ACT")) of any of Premisys' assets
perturbation.explanation: Extending the standstill period to ten years could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law, making it unenforceable. California favors free competition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements. A ten-year standstill provision could be viewed as an unlawful restraint on the recipient's ability to engage in business activities related to Premisys.

(a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the standstill period from one year to ten years. Both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, state that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," except as provided in the chapter. A ten-year standstill period could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, violating this law, thus creating a legal contradiction by potentially voiding the agreement. The extension significantly limits the recipient's ability to conduct business, undermining California's policy favoring free competition.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will not, without the prior written consent of Premisys, disclose to any third party (except as permitted by the terms of this Agreement) the fact that any Confidential Information has been disclosed hereunder, that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction involving the parties or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto (including the status thereof); PROVIDED, HOWEVER, that Recipient (and its Representatives) may make such disclosure if, in the reasonable opinion of counsel for such party, such disclosure is required by law, regulation or any stock exchange or the Nasdaq National Market.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will not, without the prior written consent of Premisys, disclose to any third party (except as permitted by the terms of this Agreement) the fact that any Confidential Information has been disclosed hereunder, that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction involving the parties or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect thereto (including the status thereof); Recipient (and its Representatives) may NOT make such disclosure if, in the reasonable opinion of counsel for such party, such disclosure is required by law, regulation or any stock exchange or the Nasdaq National Market.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exception for legally required disclosures may conflict with SEC regulations and other laws requiring public companies to disclose material information, potentially exposing the Recipient to legal liability for non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits making false statements or omissions of material facts in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Preventing legally required disclosures could lead to violations of this rule if the information is material to investors.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original text by preventing legally required disclosures, which could violate SEC regulations. The original text allowed Recipient to make disclosures if required by law, regulation or any stock exchange or the Nasdaq National Market. The changed text explicitly states that Recipient may NOT make such disclosures if required by law. This contradiction could lead to violations of Rule 10b-5, as the Law Explanation indicates. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, prohibits making untrue statements or omitting material facts, which could occur if legally required disclosures are prevented. This constitutes a significant contradiction because it removes a vital exception for compliance with legal and regulatory obligations, potentially exposing the Recipient to legal liability for non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of litigation arising from or relating to this Agreement, the party which is found to be the prevailing party by a court of competent jurisdiction in a final, non-appealable order shall receive from the non-prevailing party the reasonable legal fees and expenses, including attorney fees, incurred by the prevailing party in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation arising from or relating to this Agreement, the party which is found to be the prevailing party by a court of competent jurisdiction in a final, non-appealable order shall receive from the non-prevailing party 10% of the legal fees and expenses, including attorney fees, incurred by the prevailing party in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the recovery of legal fees to only 10% of the actual expenses could conflict with California Civil Code Section 1717, which provides for reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party in contract actions, potentially undermining the statute's intent.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section states that reasonable attorney's fees shall be awarded to the prevailing party, while the contract amendment imposes an arbitrary 10% cap that could be lower than those reasonable fees. This alteration contradicts the statute's provision for full recovery of reasonable fees, potentially leading to enforcement issues or a finding of unconscionability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of attorney’s f
perturbation.justification: The changed text caps the recovery of legal fees at 10%, while the original text allows for the recovery of 'reasonable legal fees and expenses'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically California Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS 1717.(a), states that 'the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract... shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs.' The 10% cap directly contradicts the statute's provision for 'reasonable attorney's fees,' because 10% could easily be lower than those 'reasonable' fees. This creates a legal contradiction because the contract now imposes an arbitrary limitation that undermines the prevailing party's right to recover reasonable attorney's fees as mandated by California law.
